name: Jormungandr
description: Jormungandr, also known as the Midgard Serpent or World Serpent, is a colossal sea serpent in Norse mythology. One of the three children of Loki and the giantess Angrboða, along with the wolf Fenrir and the goddess Hel, Jormungandr was cast into the great ocean that encircles Midgard, the realm of humans, by Odin. There, it grew to such immense size that it encircled the entire world, grasping its own tail. This act symbolizes the cyclical nature of time and the interconnectedness of all things in Norse cosmology. Jormungandr's existence is intrinsically linked to Ragnarok, the Norse apocalypse. Its thrashing in the ocean is said to herald the beginning of the end, and its venomous breath will poison the land and sea. During Ragnarok, Jormungandr will rise from the depths to battle Thor, the god of thunder, in a final, catastrophic encounter. This battle is foretold to be mutually destructive, with both Jormungandr and Thor succumbing to their wounds.
subtitle: The World Serpent of Norse Mythology
appearance: Jormungandr is described as a gigantic serpent, so large that it can encircle the entire world of Midgard. Its scales are likely dark and shimmering, resembling the depths of the ocean. Some depictions portray it with a serpentine head adorned with venomous fangs and piercing eyes, reflecting its malevolent nature.
behavior: Jormungandr is generally dormant, residing in the depths of the ocean. However, its movements are significant, as they foreshadow Ragnarok. Its behavior is driven by instinct and its role in the apocalyptic prophecy. It is a creature of immense power and destruction, a force of nature rather than a sentient being with complex motivations.

keyMyths.title: The Casting of Jormungandr
keyMyths.description: Odin, fearing the potential threat posed by Loki's children, cast Jormungandr into the ocean surrounding Midgard. This act was intended to contain the serpent's power, but instead, it allowed Jormungandr to grow to an unimaginable size.

keyMyths.title: Thor's Fishing Trip
keyMyths.description: Thor attempted to catch Jormungandr during a fishing trip with the giant Hymir. He used an ox head as bait, and Jormungandr took the bait, nearly being pulled from the water. Hymir, fearing the serpent's power, cut the line, allowing Jormungandr to escape back into the depths.

keyMyths.title: Jormungandr and Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: During Ragnarok, Jormungandr will rise from the ocean, poisoning the land and sea with its venom. It will engage in a final battle with Thor, where both combatants will inflict fatal wounds upon each other, fulfilling their roles in the apocalyptic prophecy.
keyMyths.source: Prose Edda, Gylfaginning and Poetic Edda, Völuspá

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Jormungandr's origins, size, and role in Ragnarok.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Jormungandr's origins, size, and role in Ragnarok.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems that contain mythological narratives, including depictions of Jormungandr and its interactions with Thor.
sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems that contain mythological narratives, including depictions of Jormungandr and its interactions with Thor.

sources.author: H.R. Ellis Davidson
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A scholarly analysis of Norse mythology, exploring the symbolism and cultural significance of Jormungandr.
sources.text: A scholarly analysis of Norse mythology, exploring the symbolism and cultural significance of Jormungandr.

sources.author: John Lindow
sources.work: Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology, providing insights into the context and interpretation of Jormungandr's role in the Norse worldview.
